channel.title: The Economist - World in Brief
channel.description: Catch up quickly on the global stories that matter

item.title: World in Brief - March 01, 2026
item.description:
Iranian state television reported that Ali Khamenei, Iran’s supreme leader, was killed following American and Israeli air strikes, confirming a claim made hours earlier by Donald Trump. Mr Khamenei, who ruled Iran for more than 30 years, was at his office “carrying out tasks” when he was killed, according to Iranian reports. Earlier Mr Trump said bombing would continue and repeated a call for regime change.

Iran announced 40 days of official mourning and a seven-day national holiday to commemorate the death of Mr Khamenei. The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, the supreme leader’s praetorian guard, said the killing would only make Iran more determined to continue on its path. Crowds of Iranians took to the streets of Tehran and other cities, celebrating Mr Khamenei’s death.

Israeli officials said the strikes had also killed senior regime officials, including the commander of the IRGC, and key members of Iran’s nuclear programme. Iranian media also reported that several members of Mr Khamenei’s family were killed. Binyamin Netanyahu, Israel’s prime minister, promised “thousands more targets” would be hit. Early on Sunday Israel said it had launched another wave of strikes against Iran.

Iran fired several missiles at Israel, most of which were intercepted, though one woman in Tel Aviv was reportedly killed. Iranian missiles also struck targets across the Persian Gulf. A luxury hotel in Dubai’s ritzy Palm Island neighbourhood was hit. Bahrain’s government said Iranian missiles struck a base that had served as the headquarters of the US Navy’s Fifth Fleet. There were also explosions in Qatar, Abu Dhabi, Kuwait and Saudi Arabia.

At a special meeting of the UN Security Council, António Guterres, the secretary-general, condemned the attacks. He warned they risked igniting an uncontrollable chain of events “in the most volatile region of the world”. At the meeting, Russia said the strikes violated international law. Marco Rubio, America’s secretary of state, will speak on Saturday with his counterparts in the G7.

Hundreds of thousands of air passengers were left stranded as carriers cancelled thousands of flights across the Middle East. Huge connecting airports, including in Abu Dhabi, Dubai and Doha, were among those to ground all services. Airlines also diverted planes to avoid the skies above the region.

Iran’s navy is telling ships they are not allowed through the Strait of Hormuz, according to media reports. Roughly a third of the world’s seaborne oil travels via the waterway to the Persian Gulf. Analysts expect the price of a barrel to rise to $85-90 when markets open on Monday, up from $73 on Friday. On Saturday morning Mr Trump promised to “annihilate” the Iranian navy.

What will America and Israel strike next? 

The range of targets that America and Israel have hit since they began attacking Iran on Saturday has been wide. But the most important bomb was the one that killed Ali Khamenei, Iran’s supreme leader. After his death, the list of targets is likely to change. In the coming hours and days America and Israel will go through a process known as “battle-damage assessment” to work out what they have destroyed, whom they have killed and which sites might need follow-up bombing.

Both countries will try to hit Iranian missile-launchers as they emerge from hidden positions, with the aim of pre-empting salvoes. If Iran succeeds in killing American personnel at military bases in the Persian Gulf, Donald Trump could intensify his campaign in response. Despite the uncertainty at the top of the Islamic regime, many days of war may still lie ahead.

The threat to Gulf states

Iran wasted little time before lashing out at its Arab neighbours on Saturday. It fired missiles and drones at military bases in Bahrain, Kuwait, Qatar, Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates, all of which host American troops. Most of the attacks were swatted away by air defences. It caused more damage to civilian targets: drones hit Kuwait’s international airport and a residential tower in Bahrain; a luxury hotel in Dubai was set ablaze.

Gulf rulers had spent the past two months trying to convince Donald Trump not to strike Iran, fearing exactly this sort of retaliation. Oman tried to mediate talks over a nuclear deal that would prevent war. Those efforts failed. They are desperate to avoid a prolonged war, which could shatter their reputation as safe hubs for business and tourism. Some firms are already drawing up plans in case they have to evacuate staff. Yet the harder Iran hits Gulf states, the more it may end up pushing them into alignment with America.

Sign up to enjoy a mind-expanding mix of stories, delivered six days a week

The war’s impact on energy prices

When Iran and Israel exchanged missiles last June, global oil prices jumped by 7%. The current conflict may have an even greater impact.

There are already reports that Iran’s Revolutionary Guard Corps are stopping ships passing through the Strait of Hormuz to the Persian Gulf. A complete closure of the waterway, through which 31% of all seaborne crude travels, could rapidly reduce supply worldwide. Meanwhile explosions have been heard on Kharg Island, Iran’s main oil-export terminal.

Analysts expect the price of a barrel to rise to $85-90 when markets open on Monday, up from $73 on Friday. Further disruption is possible. The Organisation of the Petroleum Exporting Countries may help matters by increasing production elsewhere; its members will meet on Sunday. But the longer the conflict drags on, the more likely it is to send energy markets reeling.

“I’m glad you like adverbs—I adore them; they are the only qualifications I really much respect.”

Henry James
